title: It was Yesterday, Apparently
description: Shimamura's birthday passed unnoticed again. Yashiro leads her to Adachi, and they share a quiet moment of forgetting—and remembering—together.
content:
So—It turns out my birthday was forgotten again this year.

Not that I can complain; even I only realized it the day after. Why is that? I'm starting to think it's not just that I have a weak presence—maybe people's recognition of me is being actively interfered with. Who even has the time to do that?

This year, I even wrote 'birthday' in small letters on the calendar. Maybe it was the extra 'THAT'S RIGHT!' someone had scribbled beside it that cursed me. Yeah—probably that. No evidence. Just a feeling.

'What do you think?' I asked while stretching the culprit's cheek.

'Fufufu, who can say?' She didn't say anything, of course, yet still managed to act all mysterious, pretending she knew something. I let go.

Today, Yashiro was dressed as a goat.

'Oho, so it was your birthday, Shimamura-san?'

'It already passed, though.'

'Ah, so a joyous occasion?'

'More or less.'

'Then donuts are in order for celebration!'

She gave a thoughtful nod and, as if it were obvious, decided what was 'birthday-appropriate.'

'What, you're buying them?'

'Ha-ha-ha! Shimamura-san, one must consider who one's asking before asking!'

'Don't get cocky.'

I tugged her cheek again, enjoyed her muffled protests for a bit, then decided to go buy some myself. Still in my school uniform, fresh from class, but that was fine. I grabbed my wallet—and Yashiro.

'So, how old are you this time?' she asked, suddenly serious.

'Let's not talk about a woman's age,' I said. A birthday is just a birthday. That's enough.

'Fair point,' she said easily, and I carried her like a cat as we headed for the donut shop near the station.

The most impressive thing about my birthday is that even Adachi—who knows me better than I do—manages to forget it. It's almost like a curse, honestly. A kind of spell that blurs my existence around this time of year.

Still, even if my birthday fades from memory, I keep getting older, inching closer to adulthood. Somehow, that feels unfair.

Time is only a measurement we humans invented, I know that. But still.

Anyway, we arrived at the donut shop. As I looked through the glass case, choosing what to buy, the happiest person in the room—without question—was the little goat.

'Feels more like it's your celebration,' I said.

'That's quite all right too,' she replied, with fake profundity.

'Don't try to sound deep.'

The bag of donuts and Yashiro's short legs swung in sync as we walked. Come to think of it, when is her birthday? Does she even have one?

'Birthday…' she murmured suddenly.

Before I could ask, she slipped from my arms, landing neatly with the donut bag in hand.

'Shimamura-san, you should head that way.' She pointed—a direction completely opposite of my route home, toward the crowd.

'That's not even close to my house.'

'Hohoho! A little gift from me.'

'I'll go on ahead!' she said cheerfully, waving as she dashed off—light as ever, her feet barely touching the ground. People stared, but she vanished quickly.

'...Hmm.' Well, would the donuts make it home safely? Maybe not.

Still, I decided to go the way she'd pointed. Yashiro glowed faintly sometimes, but she was—more or less—my friend. And when she said things like that, it usually meant something good was waiting.

So I walked, wondering how far I'd have to go before I saw it—and then I did.

In the distance, I caught sight of Adachi's back, riding her bike. I thought it was her, anyway. She was far ahead, and I doubted I could catch up. The distance kept growing.

Still, maybe it really was her. I picked up my pace. I hesitated to shout in the middle of the crowd, hand cupped near my mouth—but, well, Adachi was getting farther away.

Before I could even call out, Adachi suddenly turned around—sharp, like she'd sensed me. She hit the brakes hard, spun the bike, and came rushing back toward me.

I was the one caught off guard. How did she even know? Does she have eyes on the back of her head?

Yashiro, Adachi—my life is just full of strange beings.

She slid to a stop, a bit out of breath, sweat glistening under the warmth of the day. I laughed quietly. Ah, I thought, so this is the 'gift' Yashiro mentioned.

'Shi–Shimamura, what are you doing here?'

'What are you doing here?' I shot back.

'No, I mean… you were smiling,' she said, still catching her breath.

'Was I?'

Adachi nervously checked her hair and clothes, like she thought something was off.

I was about to explain—then looked away. A person who's aged another year, yet still can't be honest.

'I was just buying donuts. For my birthday, apparently.'

'Birthday…' she echoed softly, lips forming the word. Then she suddenly jumped—bike clattering beside her.

'Your birthday?!'

'Yup. Yay.'

For a moment, I was older than her. Adachi quickly counted on her fingers, tilting her head. Then, cautiously:

'It was… yesterday, right?'

'Oh, you remembered. Good job.'

Her face went pale—then immediately flushed red, boiling over like a kettle. There's probably no Japanese word for that. Her emotional rollercoaster could be hazardous to her health.

In the end, she drooped, like a dog with its ears down.

'Sorry. I forgot.'

'It's fine. I forgot too.'

'W–what do you mean?'

'I mean exactly that. I just… kinda forgot it was my birthday.'

I laughed at myself, and Adachi frowned, gripping her handlebars tighter. The veins on her hands stood out.

'Then from next year on, I'll remember. I will.'

'Oh?'

'Even if you forget, I'll be here. S–so it's fine!'

She puffed herself up, awkwardly proud. When I just watched her, she cracked a clumsy little smile—so helplessly earnest that I couldn't help but warm up inside.

Yeah, maybe I was already smiling.

'That's reassuring,' I said. Then, teasingly:

'But if you forget next time?'

Adachi froze. Her face drained of color again, every emotion vanishing at once—and I instantly regretted asking. She looked ready to say something extreme.

Before she could, I placed my hand over hers on the handlebar.

We gripped it together—squeeze, squeeze.

'If Adachi forgets, then I'll remember. That makes it even.'

Her face melted into a smile again, all soft and warm like butter.

Yeah. That felt right.

If Adachi might forget, then I have to remember. As long as that thought stays with me, maybe next year—I'll finally celebrate my birthday on the day it actually comes.
